key_insight: PTFE spray coating improved thickness and surface finish across all tested substrates, with Inconel 600 delivering the best overall mechanical and corrosion-related performance.
engineering_relevance: The study supports PTFE-coated substrates as a practical route to boost durability, reduce maintenance, and extend service life in high-temperature and corrosion-prone industrial components.
method: Surface-prepared Mild Steel, Aluminum Alloy, and Inconel 600 samples by cleaning, sandblasting, grinding, and wire brushing.
Applied PTFE using spray coating in single-coat and multi-coat formats, then cured at 380–410 °C.
Evaluated coating thickness, tensile strength, hardness, roughness, microstructure, and salt spray corrosion resistance using ASTM/IS-based tests.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports that multi-coat PTFE produced thicker coatings, roughness improved after coating, and microstructural inspection showed uniform deposition without major porosity or segregation.
watch_item: Results are constrained by short-duration salt spray testing and the absence of wear testing, so long-term tribological and corrosion performance remains uncertain.
paper_title: Investigation of Mechanical Properties of Various Substrate Materials using Polytetrafluoroethylene Coating Technique
